item.title: China Factory Activity Worsens in Warning Sign for Economy
item.description: China’s factory activity slowed in May as disruptions from a five-day break added to pressures on global demand and input costs from the continuing conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: SpaceX, OpenAI Windfall Fuels Bets on Next-Wave Asian AI Winners
item.description: The hunt is on for companies that could benefit from the tailwinds of an unprecedented wave of stock offerings in the US, and investors are increasingly honing in on the Asian supply chain.

item.title: IMF Chief, Venezuelan Officials Hold Talks on Economic Stability
item.description: International Monetary Fund Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva and Venezuelan economic official Calixto Ortega held talks in Washington, the IMF head’s first in-person meeting with country’s authorities since the fund resumed formal engagement with Venezuela last month.

item.title: US Jobs Report Set to Reveal Solid Growth, Steady Unemployment Rate
item.description: Jobs week is coming up in the US, with a whole slate of indicators on the state of the labor market culminating on Friday with the government’s official report on employment for the month of May.

item.title: Brazil Extends Measures to Limit Fuel Price Hikes by Two Months
item.description: The Brazilian government extended by two months measures to contain the rise in fuel prices triggered by the conflict in Middle East.

item.title: Paramount Is Pulling Every Lever to Sell LBO Debt
item.description: Paramount Skydance Corp. stretched, then stretched, then stretched again in its audacious $110 billion takeover bid for Warner Bros. Discovery Inc.

item.title: SoftBank to Invest Some €75 Billion in AI in France, Reports Say
item.description: SoftBank Group Corp. plans to invest as much as €75 billion ($87 billion) to build artificial intelligence data centers in France, La Tribune and the Financial Times reported.

item.title: Vitol Awarded Namibia Fuel Supply Deal From July to September
item.description: Namibia said it arranged an emergency deal with Vitol Group for three months of fuel supply through September in an effort to mitigate price shocks for oil products caused by the Iran war.

item.title: BP’s High-Stakes Reboot Descended Into an Ugly Boardroom Drama
item.description: When Albert Manifold was hired as chairman of BP Plc, he joked to friends the company needed a “gurrier” — Irish slang for a savvy and scrappy streetfighter — to help turn its fortunes around.

item.title: Fed’s Williams Quips Economist Jobs Are Safe as AI Shift Unfolds
item.description: The implications of artificial intelligence gripped global central bankers in Iceland this week, with one Federal Reserve official joking that it won’t put economists out of business.

item.title: ‘Dinosaur’ Tech Stocks Reborn as AI Fuels $1.7 Trillion Rally
item.description: They were all stars of the dot-com era before fading into the background as the bubble burst and a new generation of tech darlings rose to the forefront. But Dell Technologies Inc., Nokia Oyj and Lenovo Group Ltd. are back with a vengeance thanks to the unrelenting artificial intelligence spending boom.

item.title: Aluminum Under Pressure, While Poland Surges Ahead
item.description: It will take a long time to bring aluminum back.

item.title: AI Dangers Eclipse Nuclear Weapons at Singapore Defense Forum
item.description: The dangers of AI eclipsed those of nuclear weapons at a defense forum discussion on strategic stability in Singapore, as panelists warned it could reduce reaction times to the point where people make rash decisions.

item.title: BOE’s Mann Warns ‘Good Luck’ of Low Inflation Era Has Run Out
item.description: Bank of England rate-setter Catherine Mann warned that the long run of “good luck” central bankers have enjoyed in keeping a lid on inflation has run out as a more shock-prone era sets in.

item.title: French Finance Minister Says S&P Confirmed A+ Credit Rating
item.description: French Finance Minister Roland Lescure said S&P Global Ratings maintained the country’s credit rating, adding the government remains fully committed to continuing to reducing the public deficit and debt.

item.title: Croatian Inflation Expected to Ease in May, ECB’s Vujcic Says
item.description: Croatia’s inflation is likely to ease in May from April’s 5.8%, which was the highest in the euro area on an annual basis, outgoing central bank Governor Boris Vujcic told reporters on Saturday.

item.title: China Pledges to Retaliate If EU Imposes Trade Restrictions
item.description: China warned it would “resolutely” retaliate if the European Union proceeds with new restrictive trade measures, following a May 29 European Commission discussion on China policy.

item.title: Why Putin’s Macro Discipline Is Squeezing Russia’s Main Economic Engine
item.description: His early success in diversifying beyond oil and gas may be having unintended consequences.

item.title: Anthropic Cuts Unauthorized Platform List by Half After Pushback
item.description: Anthropic PBC updated its warning about secondary markets for its shares, cutting the number of unauthorized platforms by half, after the notice caused panic among investors and a sharp rebuttal from one of the companies named.

item.title: Hanwha Aerospace Eyes Germany, UK Arms Deals as Demand Surges
item.description: Hanwha Aerospace Co. is in talks with Germany, the UK and other European nations for new weapons deals, seeking to capitalize on a massive wave of global arms spending triggered by conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East.

item.title: US, UK and Australia Deepen Subsea Security Pact With Drones
item.description: The Aukus alliance members — Australia, UK and the US — announced Saturday that they will develop advanced underwater drone systems and further deepen their maritime relationship that will see American submarines in Australia by next year.

item.title: Charting the Global Economy: Inflation Hits Incomes and Spending
item.description: US consumer spending merely edged up in April as war-driven inflation pressures sapped incomes and pushed the saving rate to an almost four-year low.

item.title: India Says Inflation Outlook Needs Vigilance Ahead of RBI Policy
item.description: India needs to be vigilant on the inflation outlook in the country, officials at India’s finance ministry said just days ahead of a monetary policy decision by the country’s central bank, highlighting risks from a sub-par monsoon and recent fuel price hikes.

item.title: How the Invention of Rope Gave us Modern Civilization
item.description: One of the great general purpose technologies.

item.title: Odd Lots: How Rope Gave us Modern Civilization (Podcast)
item.description: Rope is easy to take for granted. It seems obvious and straightforward. But of course, it had to be invented. Early humans discovered that by twisting fibers around each other, the resulting structure would be something durable and strong. Without rope, all kinds of things aren’t possible, from lifting objects into the air to whaling or modern bridges. So how was it developed and what were the big breakthroughs in its history? On this episode, we speak with Tim Queeney, the author of the recent

item.title: Qatar Says Temporary Toll at Strait of Hormuz Is Negotiable
item.description: Qatar opposes permanent legal fees for transit through the Strait of Hormuz, but a temporary toll is negotiable and could help restore normal passage through the key waterway, a top official said.

item.title: Norway Struggles to Diversify Its Economy Hooked on Oil and Gas
item.description: Higher fuel prices in the wake of the Middle East war are reducing incentives to find other sources of economic growth

item.title: MiniMax Plans China IPO as it Eyes Local Rivals Like DeepSeek
item.description: China’s MiniMax Group Inc. has begun preparations for a domestic initial public offering, according to a regulatory filing, as the fast‑growing artificial intelligence startup challenges local rivals including DeepSeek.

item.title: Philippine Central Bank Says Inflation May Surge Further in May
item.description: The Philippine central bank said the pace of inflation is likely to increase this month, mainly because of food-price increases and the peso’s weakness.

item.title: Social Media Giants to Pay $27 Million in School Suit Accord
item.description: The world’s biggest social media platforms agreed to pay about $27 million to settle a lawsuit filed by a rural Kentucky school district that alleged their products are addictive and helped create a teen mental health crisis that drained school resources.
